coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots as a ‘green’ reagent to produce shape and size controlled gold nanoparticles for application in pollutant degradation
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Zhenzhen
Giles, Luke W.
Teo, Boon M.
Tabor, Rico F.
coredata.dc:description: Gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) have attracted wide attention as catalysts for in situ degradation of organic pollutants. Current synthetic methods that enable precise control of Au NPs size and shape cause adverse effects on the environment. In this work, a sustainable, one-step method was used to synthesize Au NPs by reducing Au3+ with norepinephrine and ethylamine-based carbon dots (NE/EA CDs). CDs not only act as reducing agent but also stabilize the resulting Au NPs. The mechanism of Au3+ reduction was investigated by studying the effects of functional groups present in the CDs, which were confirmed from UV–Vis and XPS spectral analysis. Three different morphologies of Au NPs were observed after reduction: nanotriangles, nanourchins and nanospheres. Fabricated Au NPs were used to study a model catalytic reaction, reduction of 4-nitrophenol, indicating that CDs are an attractive option for fabrication of Au NPs for water treatment applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gold nanourchins
Carbon dots
Norepinephrine
Catalytic reduction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Application of Au nanostructures for catalytic reduction of 4-nitrophenol

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the use of facilities administered by Monash University X-Ray platform and Monash Centre for Electron Microscopy.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) UV–Vis Spectra of EA CDs, EA/Ph CDs, NE/EA CDs, NE CDs and CA CDs. b) XPS survey spectra of CDs. c) High resolution C1s, N1s and O1s scans for the 5 different CD samples.

a) Absorbance of Au NPs reduced at pH 2, 6 and 10. b) Au nanotriangles reduced by CDs at pH 2. c) Au nanourchins reduced by CDs at pH 6. d) Au nanospheres reduced by CDs at pH 10, 5 μg/mL CDs solution and 100 mM Au were used here.

a) Size and zeta potential of Au NUs at different concentration of HAuCl4 solution as analysed by DLS and PALS. b) size distribution of Au NUs at different concentrations of HAuCl4 solution analysed by DLS. c–e) Au Nus when the concentration of HauCl4 is 10 mM, 50 mM and 100 mM.

a) reduction kinetics of Au3+ ions when reduced by different CDs. b–e) Representative TEM images of Au NPs reduced by b). CA CDs. c) EA CDs, d) EA/Ph CDs, e) NE CDs, for a constant concentration of HAuCl4 of 100 mM and concentration of CDs of 5 μg/mL at pH 6.

Time dependent UV–Vis spectral changes of the reaction mixture of: a) 4-nitrophenol, NaBH4 and Au NPs, and b) 4-nitrophenol and NaBH4 only. c) Plot of ln(A/A 0) versus reaction time in the presence of Au NTs (length 160 nm), Au NUs (diameter 150 nm), Au NSs (diameters 150 nm) and control group has no Au NPs. d) Plot of ln(A/A 0) versus reaction time in the presence of Au NUs with size 50, 100 and 150 nm and control with no Au NPs.

row.entry: 0.3 mM NE + 5 mM phenethylamine aqueous solution
0.3 mM NE +0.5 mM EA aqueous solution

head.ce:title: Carbon dots as a ‘green’ reagent to produce shape and size controlled gold nanoparticles for application in pollutant degradation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, Monash University, Clayton, VIC 3800, Australia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry, Monash University, Clayton VIC 3800, Australia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) have attracted wide attention as catalysts for in situ degradation of organic pollutants. Current synthetic methods that enable precise control of Au NPs size and shape cause adverse effects on the environment. In this work, a sustainable, one-step method was used to synthesize Au NPs by reducing Au3+ with norepinephrine and ethylamine-based carbon dots (NE/EA CDs). CDs not only act as reducing agent but also stabilize the resulting Au NPs. The mechanism of Au3+ reduction was investigated by studying the effects of functional groups present in the CDs, which were confirmed from UV–Vis and XPS spectral analysis. Three different morphologies of Au NPs were observed after reduction: nanotriangles, nanourchins and nanospheres. Fabricated Au NPs were used to study a model catalytic reaction, reduction of 4-nitrophenol, indicating that CDs are an attractive option for fabrication of Au NPs for water treatment applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Phenols are considered as priority pollutants by the US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) due to their high toxicity toward numerous organisms and their long persistence in the environment [1]. In particular, 4-nitrophenol is one of the most common organic pollutants in agricultural and industrial wastewaters, and has adverse effects on human beings including liver damage, protein degeneration, pancreas damage and high blood pressure [2,3]. To minimize nitrophenol pollution from wastewater, various methods have been developed to remove 4-nitrophenol, such as microwave-assisted catalytic oxidation, electrochemical treatment, chemical precipitation, chemical degradation and membrane separation [4–7]. Among these techniques, in situ degradation of 4-nitrophenol is preferred by many researchers compared to separation-based methods that are challenging and costly to deploy at large scale. In situ methods have the potential to be energy-saving, cost-effective, environmentally friendly and highly effective. Besides, as the degradation product of 4-nitrophenol, 4-aminophenol (4-AP) is not only substantially less toxic (and not a priority pollutant), but is also of great commercial value and can be used as a potent intermediate to synthesize antipyretics and analgesic drugs including phenacetin and paracetamol [8]. So, it is imperative to explore an efficient method to reduce 4-nitrophenol into 4-AP for both chemical manufacturing and environmental remediation.

Over decades of research, noble metal nanostructures have received tremendous attention for their great potential to catalyse a vast array of reactions, including the reduction of 4-nitrophenol to 4-AP [9–11]. Compared with diverse noble metal catalysts including Pt, Pd and Ag, gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) have been shown to be one of the most promising catalysts due to their chemical stability [12]. However, the catalytic performance of Au relates directly to their size, shape and dispersion and due to the high surface energy of gold, Au NPs tend to aggregate easily and decrease their catalytic activity [13]. In order to overcome these drawbacks, effective strategies to synthesize shape and size controlled Au nanostructures with high stability are in great demand.

To date, Au NPs are generally synthesized by a two-step seeding growth approach which includes in situ reduction of chloroauric acid (HAuCl4) with reducing agents followed by encapsulation with surfactants or polymers [14–16]. Most of the procedures reported in the literature use “non-green” reagents such as sodium borohydride or hydrazine as reducing agents, which are toxic and environmentally unfriendly [17,18]. Moreover, the shape directing agents used, including cetyl-trimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) are toxic and can adsorb strongly onto the surface of Au NPs, making them difficult to remove, which limits their application [19]. Traditional two-step synthesis methods require intricate controls during the process which also makes them quite expensive. Therefore, it is vitally important to pursue an efficient and surfactant-free, economic and sustainable route to yield Au NPs with controlled morphology and size.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a new kind of carbon nanomaterial consisting of carbonaceous fragments only a few nanometers in lateral dimension that are decorated with abundant functional groups [20]. They are commonly synthesized via hydrothermal treatment of precursors with no toxic reagents involved during the process [21]. Resulting CDs can act as electron acceptors and electron donors simultaneously and thus have the potential to reduce metal salts to their corresponding metals by acting as oxidation or reduction agents [22], including for the formation of gold nanoparticles [23,24]. The reactivity of CDs can be modified by doping with heteroatoms including sulfur, boron and nitrogen [25]. Nitrogen as a dopant for CDs can particularly influence their chemical and spectroscopic properties, resulting in remarkable functional changes, such as enhanced biocompatibility, photoluminescence, and catalytic activity [26–29].

In our previous work, we reported the synthesis of nitrogen-doped CDs by applying norepinephrine (NE) and ethylenediamine (EA) as precursors. The fabricated NE/EA CDs offered high photoluminescence and great biocompatibility [30]. In the present work, we report a facile method to synthesize shape and size controllable Au NPs using NE/EA CDs. NE/EA CDs can simultaneously act as a reductant to convert Au3+ to Au and a stabilizer to cap Au NPs, which simplifies the fabrication process of Au NPs and reduces material and energy cost [31]. Crucially, the morphology of Au NPs can be modified from nanotriangles (NTs) to nanourchins (NUs) and nanospheres (NSs) by changing the pH of the reaction environment. CDs derived from citric acid (CA), phenethylamine (Ph) and EA were prepared as control groups to understand the mechanism of reduction. The size of Au NPs can also be controlled by changing the concentration of Au3+. Further, fabricated Au NPs were applied as excellent catalyst for the reduction of 4-nitrophenol into 4-AP, noting considerable effects of Au NP shape and size on their catalytic activity.

After synthesis of CDs samples from diverse precursors, UV–Vis spectrophotometry and XPS were employed to compare the elemental compositions of the five different CDs (i.e. EA derived CDs (EA CDs), CA derived CDs (CA CDs), NE derived CDs (NE CDs), EA and phenethylamine derived CDs (EA/Ph CDs) and NE, EA derived CDs (NE/EA CDs)). TEM was conducted to confirm the formation of CDs as depicted in Fig. S1. UV–Vis spectra confirmed the existence of functional groups showing indicative absorbance peaks at specific wavelengths as shown in Fig. 1a. For NE/EA CDs, absorbance maxima at 230 and 287 nm are characteristic of aromatic CC sp2 hybridized carbon and C-OH hydroxyl groups, which could arise from benzene and phenol groups. And a shoulder at 380 nm is attributed to transitions of CO bonds [32]. Similarly, EA/Ph CDs show absorption peak at 265 and 355 nm, which identify the existence of benzene group. Only one absorption peak at 355 nm was present for EA CDs and CA CDs, confirming that there are only nonaromatic functional groups on these two CDs, dominated by hydroxyl groups. FTIR spectra were recorded to gain further insights into the types of functional groups present on the surface of CDs as in Fig. S2. Peaks at 1600–1200 cm−1 indicate the presence of aromatic carbon. The absorptions at 1300–1500 cm−1 are related to the bending vibration of NH and CH.

XPS measurements were performed to determine the composition of as-prepared CDs. The XPS survey spectra (Fig. 1b) reveal the existence of C, O and N elements, and it could be observed that CA CDs have the fewest N atoms present (depicted in Table S1). High resolution spectra of C1s, N1s and O1s scans for five different CDs are presented in Fig. 1c. The C1s spectra of materials showed three different bonding environments corresponding to C atoms: CC / CH/ CC (~284 eV), COH / CN (~286 eV) and CO (~288 eV). The N1s spectra showed the clear indication of the presence of two nitrogenic species: pyrrolic species (sp3 N in a 5-membered ring) at ~399 eV and graphitic N species (sp2 N substituted for C in a 6-membered ring) at 400–401 eV [33]. The O1s spectra showed at least two major peaks resulting from the quinone signature (CO) at ~530 eV and CO or OH moieties at 531–533 eV. The above observations confirm that synthesized CDs were functionalized with amine, hydroxyl and carboxylic/carbonyl moieties that originate from their precursors. To be more specific, both EA CDs and EA/Ph CDs present high pyrrolic N signals indicating the presence of amine groups, while almost no N signal could be found in CA CDs. In addition, the signal at binding energy 531–533 eV is significant for NE, NE/EA and CA CDs, which proves the existence of OH groups.

With abundant hydroxyl and amine groups at their surface, NE/EA CDs were capable of reducing Au3+ to Au NPs directly by incubating HAuCl4 solution with CDs at room temperature. Simultaneously during reduction, NE/EA CDs could maintain the stability of Au NPs by serving as a capping agent to coat Au NPs generated from the reduction. To understand which parameters influence the growth of Au NPs, we performed a series of experiments to systematically investigate how pH and concentration of Au3+ ions affect the morphology of resultant Au NPs.

NE/EA CDs suspension (5 μg/mL) with pH adjusted to 2, 6 and 10 were used in the preparation of gold nanoparticles (Au NPs). Morphology changes of Au NPs can be observed virtually and characterized via UV–Vis spectra as presented in Fig. 2a. By analysing the UV absorbance spectra in combination with TEM images, we can monitor the change of Au NPs visually, giving an indication of particle size and shape. However, these observations must be corroborated by electron microscopy for verification of the exact geometries produced, as indicated in Fig. 2b–d. When Au NPs were synthesized in pH = 2 solution, a combination of gold nanospheres (Au NSs) and gold nanotriangles (Au NTs) were observed, with sizes around 10 and 200 nm, respectively, as shown in Fig. 2b. Optical absorbance was observed at 350 and 650 nm which is can be assigned to Au NTs and Au NSs, respectively [34]. When pH was increased to 6, monodisperse gold nanourchins (Au NUs) were observed with diameters around 150 nm, and the absorbance peak shifted to 550 nm (Fig. 2c). Fig. 2d shows that Au NSs with size around 200 nm were present when reduction occurred in alkaline conditions (pH 10), and the absorbance peak red shifted again to 650 nm. These results demonstrate that in different pH conditions, our NE/EA based CDs allow us to obtain Au nanostructures with controlled shape in an efficient way.

Mechanistically, the change of shape of Au NPs can be explained by the shape growth being directed by the rate of Au3+ ion reduction [35]. In acidic conditions, abundant H+ ions in solution will decrease the availability of electrons that reduce gold ions, and the reaction rate becomes slower. At this slow reduction rate, gold seeds formed at the initial nucleation stage will grow into nanoplates and then proceed to form triangle structures [36]. Similarly, at high pH values, excess hydroxyl groups directly react with AuCl4 − and convert it to AuCl3(OH)−, hence slowing down the reduction speed and forming large Au NSs aggregates [37,38]. In near-neutral conditions (pH = 6–8), both amine and hydroxyl groups are available for gold binding, and thus a large number of Au complexes can bind to the Au seed and allow the subsequent formation of large number of small branches. There may also be a contribution of directed growth or capping of specific crystal faces, resulting in favoured branch growth.

Fig. 3a and b present TEM images of Au NUs produced at different concentrations of HAuCl4 at pH = 6. The size distribution and zeta potential of Au NPs were analysed using dynamic light scattering (DLS) and phase analysis light scattering (PALS). From the DLS analysis, highly monodisperse Au NUs increase in diameter when the dosage of HAuCl4 increases. Their zeta potential also increases in magnitude as size increases, indicating increased stability of Au NUs. In Fig. 3c–e, we show representative TEM images of Au NUs formed with different starting concentrations of gold ions. This confirms that on increasing the loading of Au3+ from 10 to 50 mM, and then to 100 mM, the diameter of Au NUs increases from 50 to 100 to 150 nm. DLS measurements of Au NP diameter show larger diameters than TEM, which is attributed to the fact that in Au NP dispersions, large Van der Waals forces induce attractions between Au NPs, resulting in clustering that makes the hydrodynamic size obtained by DLS appear larger.

It is reported that Au3+ can be reduced to Au0 by both hydroxyl and amine groups on the surface of CDs, which act as electron donors [39,40]. The formation of Au NPs is apparent visually as the color of solution turns from yellow to green, blue or purple (as depicted in Fig. 2a).

Kinetics curves for the reduction of Au3+ ions by the five different CDs samples are presented in Fig. 4a. After 30 min of reaction, NE/EA CDs showed the highest conversion percentage (conversion yield is defined as the percentage of gold ions reduced to gold atoms), followed by NE, EA, EA/Ph, and lastly CA CDs. CA CDs have abundant hydroxyl groups on the surface, and 5.7% of Au3+ has been reduced which aligns with the fact that hydroxyl groups are a widely-used reductant for reduction of Au3+ [41]. When there are only amine groups present on the CDs (i.e. EA CDs), the conversion percentage of Au3+ is 10.4%, which is similar to that of EA/Ph CDs at 11.6%, and demonstrates that the presence of benzene rings in the CDs structure does not influence conversion efficacy. There are both amine and hydroxyl groups in the NE CDs, and the coupled effect of these two groups increase the conversion percentage of Au3+ to 20% in the first 30 mins. When the concentration of amine groups increases further, as is the case for NE/EA CDs, the conversion percentage increases significantly to 64%.

Time was also a significant factor in the reduction process. After 24 h, CA CDs reduced almost 4 times more Au3+ than in the first half hour (conversion percentage was 23%). As for EA CDs and EA/Ph CDs, 73% of Au3+ was reduced after this time. The highest conversion efficacy was observed in NE/EA CDs at 93.7%, further indicating their effectiveness.

Based on these results, we hypothesize a possible reduction mechanism. The abundant hydroxyl groups on the surface of CDs can act as reducing agents in the redox formation of Au NPs. The resulting non-capping Au nanoseed carry a positive surface charge while CDs bear a negative charge, which causes them to bind to each other through electrostatic interactions, producing solid Au NPs [42]. Additionally, amine groups donate their lone pair to form AuN coordination bonds with undercoordinated Au atoms, capping NPs' surface, stabilizing the metal NPs and forming branches on Au NPs [43,44]. Thus the combination of amine and hydroxyl groups synergistically favors the reduction of Au3+ ions.

The differences that result from varied Au3+ reduction pathways via use of different CDs are reflected in the morphological differences between obtained Au NPs as observed using TEM (Fig. 4b–e). Hydroxyl-rich CA CDs caused slow, kinetically controlled formation of Au NPs, and resulted in large and spherical shaped NPs with diameters around 100 nm (Fig. 4b). On comparison, amine groups can bind to the surface of reduced Au NPs and act as anchors to keep reduction localized at the surface and form smaller Au NPs [41]. For CDs which have amine groups only, (i.e. EA CDs and EA/Ph CDs) they generated Au NPs that were 10 times smaller, with irregular shapes (Fig. 4c and d). NE CDs combined the functionalities of both hydroxyl and amine groups, producing medium-sized Au NPs with a spherical core but only a few branches (Fig. 4e). When the ratio of amine groups is higher, as is the case for NE/EA CDs, the surface of resulting Au NPs includes more branches, which we refer to here as urchin-shaped nanoparticles or nanourchins (Fig. 3e).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of Au nanostructures for catalytic reduction of 4-nitrophenol
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of Au-NP shape on the reduction of 4-nitrophenol with NaBH4 to form 4-AP was examined. With the addition of Au NPs to the mixture containing 4-nitrophenol and NaBH4, an evolution of a new peak for 4-AP at 300 nm raises indicating the formation of 4-AP. A gradual decrease in the characteristic absorbance peak of 4-nitrophenol at 400 nm was also observed, as indicated by the spectra shown in Fig. 5a, indicating that ultimately >99% reduction had occurred, comparable to other catalyzed nitrophenol reductions in the literature [45,46]. By comparison, 4-nitrophenol is inert to NaBH4 alone, and the reaction did not occur in the absence of Au NPs even after 24 h as indicated in Fig. 5b.

Pseudo-first-order kinetics can be applied to evaluate rate constants for nitrophenol reduction owing to the presence of excess NaBH4 in the experiment. As shown in Fig. 5c and d, by measuring the absorbance of 4-nitrophenol at 400 nm (A) with reaction time and plotting ln(A/A 0) versus reaction time (t), a linear relationship between ln(A/A 0) and reaction time (t) was obtained for all Au NPs. The rate constant can be estimated from the slope of the linear relationship of ln(A/A 0) versus reaction time.

In Fig. 5c, it is clearly seen that Au NUs present highest reaction rate constant at 0.092 min−1 which can be explained by their multibranched structure resulting in a high surface area, which could be beneficial for the adsorption of 4-nitrophenol molecules. They may also present more favourable (reactive) facets to facilitate catalysis. Au NTs and Au NSs show lower rate constants at 0.044 min−1 and 0.059 min−1, which may be attributed to their relatively lower surface area and fewer (or no) branches resulting in fewer and less diverse facets.

As shown in Fig. 5d, the reduction rate of 4-nitrophenol increases from 0.092 min−1 to 0.118 and 0.179 min−1 as the Au NPs diameter decreases from 150 to 50 nm. The increase in rate constant can be primarily explained by the increased relative surface area. Additionally, it was reported that a decrease in particle size leads to an increase in the fraction of low coordination metal sites at edges, which can prompt the adsorption of reactants and facilitate the reaction.

In summary, we demonstrated a one-step, shape and size controllable synthetic method for the preparation of Au NPs using CDs. It was found that hydroxyl groups tend to form Au nanospheres with large diameter (100 nm), while amine groups favor formation of smaller Au nanospheres with size around 10 nm. Urchin shaped nanoparticles are formed if hydroxyl and amine groups present together. The size of Au NPs could be increased when the concentration of HAuCl4 was increased. Moreover, fabricated Au NPs all exhibited high catalytic activity toward 4-nitrophenol reduction at room temperature. However, catalytic efficacy exhibited shape- and size-dependent properties, and the highest rate was observed using Au NUs with size 50 nm. Our findings provide a facile and environmentally friendly way to control the morphology of Au NPs and suggest that CD reduced Au NPs can be developed for catalytic degradation of recalcitrant contaminants in wastewater.

Ethylenediamine (99%, CAS number 107–15-3), L-norepinephrine hydrochloride (CAS number: 329–56-6), phenethylamine (2-phenylethylamine, 99%, CAS number 64–04-0), citric acid, hydrogen tetrachloroaurate (III) hydrate, sodium hydroxide, hydrochloric acid, 4-nitrophenol, and sodium borohydride were purchased from Sigma Aldrich and used without further purification.
A range of different CDs from diverse precursors were synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal method. The precursors for different CDs are listed in Table 1 .

Typically, 5 mL of solution was transferred into a Teflon®-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 8 h. The autoclave was cooled to room temperature after this time, and the resultant solution was centrifuged at 5000 ×g for 20 min to collect the pellet, discarding the supernatant. The pellet was collected and freeze-dried for long term storage. CDs were not purified by dialysis due to their small size, which makes this process challenging and inefficient.

In a typical preparation, 1 mL of 5 μg/mL CDs were mixed with 1 mL of 10, 50 and 100 mM HAuCl4 aqueous solution at room temperature for 24 h. To test the effect of pH, a separate set of experiments were performed with variation of the pH of both HAuCl4 aqueous solution and NE/EA CDs solution including pH = 2, 6 and 10. pH of HAuCl4 aqueous solution and NE/EA CDs solution was adjusted by addition of small volumes of 1 M NaOH or HCl solution. 1 mL of 50 mM HAuCl4 aqueous solution was mixed with 1 mL of 5 μg/mL NE/EA CDs at different pH conditions. Mixtures were left at room temperature for 24 h before being centrifuged at 15,000 rpm for 15 mins, each time retaining the pellet and discarding the supernatant. The resulting pellet was washed three times with ultrapure water, centrifuging between each wash. Finally, the pellet was collected for further characterization.

To investigate the morphological differences between AuNPs reduced using different CDs, 1 mL of 50 mM HAuCl4 aqueous solution was mixed with 1 mL of 5 μg/mL EA CDs, EA/Ph CDS, NE CDs and CA CDs separately at pH 6. Mixtures were kept at room temperature for 24 h before being centrifuged at 15,000 g for 15 mins and washed three times with ultrapure water (as above). Finally, the pellet was collected for further characterization.

The size and dispersity of Au NPs were characterized using dynamic light scattering (DLS) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 T20 electron microscope under 200 kv, using LaB6 emitter). UV–vis spectra of the CDs were obtained using a Cary 60 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies).

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on a Thermo Scientific Nexsa Surface Analysis System equipped with a hemispherical analyser. The incident radiation was monochromatic Al Kα X-rays (1486.6 eV) at 72 W (6 mA and 12 kV, 400 × 800 μm2 spot). Survey (wide) and high-resolution (narrow) scans were recorded at analyser pass energies of 150 and 50 eV and step sizes of 1.0 eV and 0.1 eV, respectively. The base pressure in the analysis chamber was less than 5.0 × 10−9 mbar. A low-energy dual-beam (ion and electron) flood gun was used to compensate for surface charging. Data processing was carried out using Avantage software and the energy calibration was referenced to the main line of C 1 s at 284.8 eV. The background used for curve fitting was “Smart”. Samples were mounted using carbon tape to affix the powdery samples to the sample stage.

After mixing 100 mM HAuCl4 solution with CDs, AuNPs were found in all mixtures. After a given time interval as noted, mixtures were centrifuged at 9000 xg for 15 mins and the supernatant was collected to quantify the concentration of unreacted Au3+ by comparing to an obtained Au standard curve at wavelength of 220 nm (Fig. S3a and b). The conversion percentage of Au was presented as (C original – C after reduction)/C original *100%.

The conversion of 4-nitrophenol to 4-aminophenol (4-AP) with sodium borohydride (NaBH4) was explored to investigate the catalytic ability of Au NPs reduced by NE/EA CDs. First, 2 mL of ultrapure water, 20 μL of 10 mM 4-nitrophenol aqueous solution, and 200 μL of freshly prepared 100 mM NaBH4 were added into a standard 1 cm path-length quartz spectrophotometry cuvette. Then 100 μL of 0.1 mg/mL Au NPs solution was added. Progress of the reduction reaction was monitored at room temperature using UV–Vis spectrophometry. The rate of reduction was calculated by measuring the time-dependent change in absorbance of the solution at 400 nm.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the use of facilities administered by Monash University X-Ray platform and Monash Centre for Electron Microscopy.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colcom.2021.100571.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hierarchical structure based on Pd (au) nanoparticles grafted onto magnetite cores and double layered shells: enhanced activity for catalytic applications
J. Mater. Chem. A

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wang Q, Jia W, Liu B, Dong A, Gong X, Li C, Jing P, Li Y, Xu G, Zhang J.
